agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quote lifecycle. The term 'confirmed' is already established in the Order bounded context with a specific, well-defined meaning that includes payment completion and immutability. Reusing this term in the Quote context with a fundamentally different meaning creates a dangerous domain language collision.","bounded_context":"Quote Management (proposed change) vs. Order Fulfillment (existing definition)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"ESTABLISHED: An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (PROPOSED)","definition":"PROPOSED: A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms, but payment has NOT occurred. Mutability is unspecified."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A pricing proposal presented to a customer, subject to acceptance or rejection."},{"term":"Verbal Agreement","definition":"A non-binding customer indication of intent to proceed, distinct from a contractual commitment backed by payment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The term 'confirmed' in the Order context means paid and immutable.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an established domain invariant — 'confirmed' on an order guarantees payment has occurred and state cannot change. Violating this corrupts fulfillment integrity.","testable_assertion":"Every entity with status 'confirmed' in the Order context has a completed payment record and rejects all mutation attempts."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A verbal agreement does not constitute payment and must not trigger fulfillment workflows.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical/financial constraint — you cannot fulfill what has not been paid for. Triggering fulfillment on a verbal agreement creates financial exposure.","testable_assertion":"No fulfillment workflow is initiated for any entity that lacks a completed payment record, regardless of status name."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Domain terms must have exactly one meaning within the system's ubiquitous language to prevent ambiguity across bounded contexts.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a DDD best practice and team policy, not a physical constraint. However, violating it in this specific case is high-risk because 'confirmed' spans contexts that interact directly (quotes become orders)."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer writes a cross-cutting query for all 'confirmed' entities and inadvertently mixes unpaid quotes with paid orders.","expected_behavior":"This must be impossible. The system should not allow a single status value to mean 'paid and immutable' in one context and 'verbal agreement, unpaid' in another."},{"scenario":"A fulfillment automation triggers on status='confirmed' and picks up a quote that has no payment.","expected_behavior":"Fulfillment must never activate without verified payment. Sharing the term 'confirmed' across Quote and Order creates a direct vector for this defect."},{"scenario":"Customer verbally agrees (quote 'confirmed'), then disputes. Sales agent needs to modify terms.","expected_behavior":"The quote must remain mutable since no payment has occurred. But 'confirmed' in the existing domain means immutable — developers will reasonably apply the immutability invariant to quotes as well, locking out legitimate edits."},{"scenario":"A sales report shows 'confirmed' items and stakeholders assume revenue is secured.","expected_behavior":"'Confirmed' quotes represent zero secured revenue. Mixing them with confirmed orders in reporting will inflate revenue projections and mislead finance."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK — If 'confirmed' quotes are conflated with confirmed orders in reporting or automation, the business may report unsecured revenue or trigger fulfillment for unpaid work. Both create direct financial exposure.","operational":"HIGH RISK — Every developer, QA engineer, and ops person who encounters 'confirmed' will need to ask 'confirmed in which context?' This cognitive overhead compounds across the entire team and degrades over time.","user":"MODERATE RISK — Sales agents get a useful workflow state, but downstream users (warehouse, finance, support) will face confusion when 'confirmed' doesn't mean what it has always meant.","risk":"CRITICAL — This is a domain language collision on a term that governs an existing invariant (immutability + payment). The blast radius extends to fulfillment, finance, reporting, and any automation that keys on status names.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Positive — they get a new workflow state to track verbal agreements. However, they may also assume 'confirmed' quotes are locked down, reducing their own flexibility."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue Team","impact":"Negative — 'confirmed' currently signals secured revenue. Introducing unpaid 'confirmed' quotes pollutes this signal."},{"group":"Warehouse / Fulfillment","impact":"Negative — any automation or manual process keyed on 'confirmed' status risks premature fulfillment of unpaid quotes."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"Negative — maintaining two contradictory definitions of 'confirmed' increases bug surface and onboarding confusion permanently."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Indirect risk — premature fulfillment or billing errors stemming from the ambiguity could degrade trust."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is defined as paid+immutable in the established domain, but the feature request redefines it as unpaid+verbal agreement. This is not an ambiguity — it is a collision between an established invariant and a proposed redefinition of the same term. Confidence is low because the feature CANNOT be accepted as specified without violating existing business rules. The intent behind the request is clear and valid; the terminology choice is the problem.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed' (paid, immutable) is established and in active use","A verbal agreement is fundamentally different from a completed payment","Reusing the same status term for contradictory meanings will cause defects"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the Quote and Order bounded contexts share a database, event bus, or status enum — this determines the blast radius","Whether existing automations or reports already key on the string 'confirmed'","What the full Quote lifecycle looks like (other statuses, transitions)"],"assumptions":["Quotes and Orders are related entities in the same system (quotes convert to orders)","The existing 'confirmed' definition is enforced in code, not just documentation","No mechanism currently exists to disambiguate status terms across bounded contexts"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The feature request redefines 'confirmed' to mean 'verbal agreement, unpaid, presumably mutable' — directly contradicting the established domain definition where 'confirmed' means 'paid, immutable, queued for fulfillment.' These two definitions cannot coexist under the same term without violating the existing invariant or creating systemic ambiguity.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Finance","Warehouse/Fulfillment","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["A: Reject the term 'confirmed' for quotes. Use a distinct term that accurately represents the business state — e.g., 'accepted', 'verbally_agreed', 'customer_approved', or 'terms_accepted'. This preserves the existing invariant and clearly communicates that payment has not occurred.","B: Accept the term 'confirmed' for quotes but formally redefine 'confirmed' as context-dependent with explicit disambiguation in code (e.g., QuoteStatus.CONFIRMED vs OrderStatus.CONFIRMED with different invariants). This adds permanent cognitive overhead and requires auditing all existing code that references 'confirmed'.","C: Accept as-is and hope contexts never collide. This is negligent and will cause defects."],"recommendation":"Option A — strongly recommended. The business state being described ('customer verbally agreed, no payment') already has natural language that is precise and unambiguous. Terms like 'accepted' or 'customer_approved' accurately convey what happened without colliding with the payment-backed meaning of 'confirmed'. The feature request's INTENT is valid; only the TERMINOLOGY is wrong."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Domain language collision: 'confirmed' is proposed with a meaning that directly contradicts its established definition.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: 'Confirmed' means paid and immutable. The proposed usage means unpaid and (implicitly) mutable."},{"violation":"Risk of premature fulfillment: any automation or logic keyed on 'confirmed' status could treat unpaid quotes as paid orders.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: A verbal agreement does not constitute payment and must not trigger fulfillment."}]}
